pages.title: Casting versus Forging
pages.text: A metal part poured into a reusable Gold Cast begins with $(bold)Crude workmanship$() and $(bold)casting porosity$(). Polishing improves its finish but cannot repair its workmanship.$(p)Hold $(bold)Shift$() over a part to inspect its manufacture, effective score, heat treatment, defects, and alloy composition. Assembled equipment retains these records for every component.

pages.title: Building a Foundry
pages.text: Place the $(bold)Foundry Forge$() in the lowest wall layer, facing away from the interior. Behind it, build a 1–9 block wide, 1–9 block deep blackstone floor with four straight blackstone wall faces.$(p)The interior must be empty, the top must remain open, and walls may be 1–8 blocks high. Corners are optional. Foundry Glass, Fuel Tanks, and Drains can replace tagged wall blocks.$(p)Use the controller to receive the exact coordinates of the first construction problem.

pages.title: Fuel and Melting
pages.text: Replace a wall block with a $(bold)Foundry Fuel Tank$(), then fill it with a registered fluid such as lava. Add metal by using it on the controller or dropping it into the interior.$(p)Each interior block holds $(bold)1,000 mB$(). The foundry reserves space for queued solids and only burns fuel hot enough for the next item. Look up Foundry Melting and Foundry Fuels in JEI or EMI for exact amounts, temperatures, and times.

pages.title: Raw Ore Processing
pages.text: Raw iron, gold, and copper produce $(bold)two ingots$() of molten metal per item. A raw storage block produces 1,620 mB, so it requires at least two interior blocks of free capacity.$(p)Processed ingots and metal storage blocks melt at their original volume. This makes the foundry valuable at the mine while preventing repeated casting and remelting from multiplying metal.

pages.title: Stoking the Foundry
pages.text: Use $(bold)Blaze Powder$() on a formed Foundry Forge to stoke it for one minute of active processing. A stoked foundry melts at $(bold)twice the speed$() and burns fuel twice as quickly, so throughput improves without changing fuel efficiency.$(p)Up to four minutes can be stored. The boost only counts down while metal is actually melting, and the controller or Jade shows the remaining time.

pages.title: Draining and Casting
pages.text: Install a $(bold)Foundry Drain$() in a wall, attach a Faucet to its outside face, and place a Casting Table or Basin directly below.$(p)An empty table pours ingots; an empty basin pours storage blocks. To make a reusable Gold Cast, place a finished part on a table and pour gold over it. Put that cast back on the table to pour crude metal parts.$(p)The bottom molten layer always pours first. Use the faucet or Jade tooltip to learn why a pour is stalled.

pages.title: Reforging a Part
pages.text: Only $(bold)uncoated, workable metal parts$() can be reforged. Handles, strings, leather, gems, casts, coatings, and assembled equipment cannot.$(p)Heat the part, select its matching pattern on a Smithing Anvil, place the hot part, then hammer it as normal. Reforging keeps its material and alloy composition, removes casting porosity, resets heat treatment, and replaces its workmanship with the new hammering result.

pages.title: Hardening and Tempering
pages.text: Quench a part from $(bold)60–89% heat$() to harden it. Quenching at $(bold)90% or hotter$() leaves it brittle.$(p)To temper a hardened or brittle part, reheat it to $(bold)30–65%$(), then let it cool completely. Tempered work gains a small effective-quality bonus; casting porosity is a small penalty, while brittleness is severe.

pages.title: Alloys
pages.text: Exact-ratio alloys react automatically inside the foundry with a sound and flame burst. Excess inputs remain in their own molten layers.$(p)$(bold)Steel:$() 90 mB iron + 10 mB carbon → 90 mB steel. Coal and charcoal each provide 10 mB carbon.$(p)$(bold)Bronze:$() 270 mB copper + 90 mB tin → 360 mB bronze. Tin uses the common $(italic)c:ingots/tin$() tag for mod compatibility. Alloy recipes are datapack-controlled.

pages.title: Metal Recovery
pages.text: Drop or insert an uncoated metal tool or armour part into a formed foundry to recycle it. It melts back into its recorded material.$(p)Recovery is intentionally below 100%. Better workmanship returns more metal, while worn parts return less. This makes casting useful for blanks and bulk production without replacing careful forging.

pages.title: Foundry Progression
pages.text: The Foundry Engineering advancement tree follows the intended route: build the controller, create a reusable gold cast, master bronze and steel, stoke the furnace with blaze powder, then complete netherite metallurgy.$(p)Bronze is the accessible bulk alloy, steel rewards carbon management, and netherite remains the late-game goal because one ingot still requires four scraps and four gold ingots.
